They could also encourage closer alignment between the implementation of resolution 2803 and other multilateral frameworks, including the New York Declaration that the General Assembly endorsed in September 2025."},{"url":"https://jstreet.org/six-months-in-assessing-the-status-of-the-gaza-ceasefire/","title":"Six Months In: Assessing the Status of the Gaza Ceasefire - J Street","content":"Six months after the October 10, 2025 announcement of a Gaza ceasefire, the 20-point peace plan announced by President Trump has largely stalled on all its promises beyond the initial pause in fighting, hostage exchange, and surge in humanitarian aid.
At the inaugural Board of Peace meeting on February 19, approximately $17 billion was pledged: $10 billion from the U.S. and $7 billion from nine countries including the UAE ($1.2B), Saudi Arabia ($1B), Kuwait ($1B), and Qatar ($1B).
Gaza will be redeveloped for the benefit of the people of Gaza, who have suffered more than enough."
More than 80 percent of all buildings in Gaza have been damaged or destroyed – generating approximately 61 million tonnes of rubble – and 90 percent of Gaza’s population is currently living in tents in the midst of that rubble."},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaza_peace_plan","title":"Gaza peace plan - Wikipedia","content":"The ceasefire went into effect on October 10, though there have been numerous attacks since, including the killing of more than 1,060 Palestinians by Israel.​ The commencement of the second phase of the plan was announced by US Special Envoy Steve Witkoff on 14 January 2026, but as of May 2026, negotiations have remained stalled due to repeated Israeli attacks since ‌the October ceasefire and disagreements over the plan of Hamas disarmament.
On October 3, Trump gave Hamas a deadline of Sunday October 5, 2025, 22:00 GMT, to accept the proposed deal.
Palestinian factions including Hamas, Islamic Jihad, Popular Front, Democratic Front, Popular Resistance Committees, National Initiative, and the Democratic Reform Movement (Dahlan Movement) started meeting in Cairo on November 24 to discuss a draft "protocol of action of the international force".
These hostages, including Asaf Hamami and Omer Neutra, were soldiers who were abducted after being killed in combat during the October 7 attacks.
The commencement of the second phase of the plan was announced by US Special Envoy Steve Witkoff on January 14, 2026 along with the formation of the National Committee for the Administration of Gaza to manage the day-to-day administration of the Gaza Strip during the transitional period.
On January 22, President Trump officially ratified the Charter of the Board of Peace during a high-profile ceremony at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

Author interview with senior Hamas leader, Doha, December 2025."},{"url":"https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/1/15/gaza-plan-phase-two-us-to-discuss-hamas-disarmament-israeli-withdrawal","title":"Gaza plan phase two: US to discuss Hamas disarmament, Israeli withdrawal | Israel-Palestine conflict News | Al Jazeera","content":"In phase two, the US and other mediators will need to tackle the disarmament of Hamas – which has refused to give up its arms while Israel occupies the territory – and the deployment of an international peacekeeping force."}]}

However, Israel waited for the completion of the","score":0.72395444,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://idsa.in/publisher/issuebrief/situation-in-gaza-post-october-2025-ceasefire","title":"Situation in Gaza post-October 2025 Ceasefire - MP-IDSA","content":"Hamas’ rejection of complete disarmament, Israel’s continued military actions and expansion of the buffer zone have led to an impasse in the Gaza peace plan’s second phase, prolonging the humanitarian crisis.*. Despite the ceasefire, progress towards tangible peace and rehabilitation has stalled due to Israel’s ongoing attacks leading to around 1,000 Palestinian deaths between October 2025 and June 2026, its refusal to withdraw from the buffer zone and its expansion from the agreed 53 per cent to 64 per cent. On 16 January 2026, Trump announced the second phase of the plan to initiate Hamas’ disarmament and dismantle its military and tunnel infrastructure, facilitate Israeli withdrawal, establish a security mechanism and technocratic government and launch reconstruction. The Board of Peace’s (BoP) Executive Board, Gaza Executive Board, National Committee for the Administration of Gaza (NCAG), and Office of High Representative for Gaza were established on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum summit in Davos on 22 January 2026.","score":0.7156829,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XlScXDQvVxM","title":"What are the hurdles to implementing phase 2 of Gaza ceasefire? | Inside Story","content":"What are the hurdles to implementing phase 2 of Gaza ceasefire? | Inside Story

We have officially entered the second phase of Gaza's 20-point peace plan." Those were the words of the US president, who said that Washington will reach a deal with Hamas on handing over its weapons. He also threatened the group with consequences if it doesn't comply. Donald Trump stopped short of calling on Israel to fulfill its commitments. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu described the US announcement as largely symbolic. So, is the transition to the next phase even possible?

Presenter: James Bays
Guest: Xavier Abu Eid, political analyst and former adviser to the Palestine Liberation Organization negotiation team.
Yezid Sayigh, Senior Fellow at the Carnegie Middle East Center.  
Yossi Beilin, former Israeli Justice Minister.
